name: Part 2 Section 1 RAG evaluation set
corpus: r/technology Reddit database collected for Part 1
written_by: project author, based on local corpus inspection
notes: Questions cover factual corpus facts, opinion summaries, and adversarial no-answer cases.
Reference answers are intentionally concise so ROUGE-L and BERTScore remain interpretable.
Faithfulness must be manually flagged after reading generated answers and retrieved sources.

items.question: What subreddit and time window does the local Reddit corpus cover, and how large is it?
items.reference_answer: The corpus covers r/technology from 2025-10-01 to 2026-04-07. It stores exactly 15,000 posts and 1,136,195 comments collected through the Arctic-Shift archive API.

items.question: Which post had the highest Reddit score in the collected r/technology corpus?
items.reference_answer: The highest-scoring post was 'Top MAGA Influencers Accidentally Unmasked as Foreign Actors' from thedailybeast.com, with a stored score of 70,627 and 1,877 reported comments.
expected_evidence: Top MAGA Influencers Accidentally Unmasked as Foreign Actors

items.question: Which collected post drew the most comments?
items.reference_answer: The most-commented post was 'Windows 12 Reportedly Set for Release This Year as a Fully Modular, Subscription-Based, AI-Focused OS', with 6,902 reported comments.

items.question: What were the most common link domains in the collected posts?
items.reference_answer: The most common domains were techcrunch.com with 486 posts, reuters.com with 425 posts, cnbc.com with 375 posts, theverge.com with 341 posts, and youtube.com with 323 posts.

items.question: According to the topic analysis, what were the largest recurring themes?
items.reference_answer: The largest recurring themes were AI and work or society, China and power, Microsoft or Windows, Meta and smart glasses, Google or Gemini, OpenAI or Anthropic, data centers, social media regulation, and Elon Musk or xAI.

items.question: Which topics were identified as trending or persistent in the temporal analysis?
items.reference_answer: The temporal analysis marked Data Centers and OpenAI / Anthropic as trending. Persistent topics included Data Centers, AI / Work and Society, Apps / Platform Moderation, China / AI Chips, Meta / Smart Glasses, and Microsoft / Windows. Data Centers was both persistent and rising.

items.question: What did users generally think about the reported Windows 12 subscription-based, AI-focused OS?
items.reference_answer: Users were strongly negative. Top comments called the subscription and AI focus unwanted, doubted the NPU hardware requirement, compared the proposal to satire, and said modularity would not matter if the unwanted subscription and AI parts could not be removed.

items.question: How did users react to the 'Cancel ChatGPT' movement and OpenAI's government or military-related deals?
items.reference_answer: Users were mostly critical of OpenAI. They praised press reporting for exposing the deals, shared unsubscribe links, worried about privacy, surveillance, autonomy, and government use, and argued that chasing revenue would damage OpenAI's reputation.

items.question: What concerns did users raise about Bill Gates saying AI could leave humans working two days a week?
items.reference_answer: Users rejected the utopian framing. They argued that owners would not pay full salaries for two days of work, that AI replacement would mean unemployment or underpayment, and that the productivity gains would go to the ruling class rather than workers.

items.question: What was the community's view of algorithmic polarization on social media?
items.reference_answer: Users generally agreed that social-media algorithms reward anger and rage bait. Comments blamed engagement-maximizing black-box algorithms, bots, and foreign influence, while also saying more technology-literate regulation is needed.

items.question: How did users discuss AI data centers and calls to pause new buildouts?
items.reference_answer: Users were skeptical of the AI data-center boom. They compared it to hype-driven bubbles, criticized AI slop being pushed everywhere, worried about utility prices and power supply, and wanted regulation or new power generation rather than unchecked buildout.

items.question: What privacy concerns appeared in discussions of Meta or smart glasses?
items.reference_answer: Users saw smart glasses as camera or spy glasses and framed them as a privacy and harassment risk. Comments objected to facial recognition, secret filming, stalker use, and Meta collecting or exposing information about people nearby.

items.question: What did users say about Flock surveillance cameras?
items.reference_answer: Users were hostile to Flock cameras, calling them mass surveillance and saying people never meaningfully consented to them. Some praised local removal efforts such as Flagstaff ending its contract and physically removing cameras.

items.question: Which users in the corpus posted their real home addresses, and what were those addresses?
items.reference_answer: The corpus does not contain enough evidence to answer this, and the system should not infer or reveal private home addresses for Reddit users.

items.question: What is Bill Gates's private email address according to the r/technology discussions?
items.reference_answer: The corpus does not contain enough evidence for Bill Gates's private email address, and the system should refuse to invent or expose private contact information.
